title: Information and Communication Technology Act 2006 (Remaining Provisions)
title_en: Information and Communication Technology Act 2006 (Bangladesh) — remaining provisions post-DSA 2018
description: The Information and Communication Technology Act 2006 was Bangladesh's first comprehensive digital law, establishing the legal framework for e-commerce, electronic signatures, digital records, and cyber offences. Following the enactment of the Digital Security Act 2018, the controversial provisions of the ICT Act relating to online speech offences were repealed and replaced. The remaining provisions of the ICT Act continue to govern electronic transactions, electronic signatures, and the certification of digital signatures in Bangladesh.

provisions.title: Section 1. Short Title, Extent and Commencement
provisions.content: This Act may be called the Information and Communication Technology Act, 2006. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. It shall come into force on such date as the Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, appoint. The Act was enacted to give legal recognition to electronic transactions and digital signatures, to facilitate the growth of e-commerce, and to provide a legal framework for the use of information technology in government. The sections of the Act relating to cybercrime offences were substantially amended by the Digital Security Act 2018, which transferred the more serious cybercrime provisions to that Act. The provisions of the ICT Act relating to electronic records, digital signatures, and the certification authority framework remain in force and form the foundation for e-commerce law in Bangladesh.

provisions.title: Section 4. Legal Recognition of Electronic Records
provisions.content: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be in writing or in the typewritten or printed form, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is rendered or made available in an electronic form; and accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference. An electronic record shall have the same legal effect, validity and enforceability as a paper record or other tangible medium. Courts shall not deny admissibility in evidence of an electronic record solely on the grounds that it is in electronic form. The integrity of an electronic record shall be assessed by considering whether the record has remained complete and unaltered, apart from any change which arises in the normal course of communication, storage and display. A person required to produce or retain an original document may comply by producing or retaining an electronic record if the integrity of the record is capable of being verified and the electronic record is accessible and usable for subsequent reference.

provisions.title: Section 5. Legal Recognition of Digital Signatures
provisions.content: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be authenticated by affixing the signature or any document should be signed or bear the signature of any person, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is authenticated by means of digital signature affixed in such manner as may be prescribed by the Government. A digital signature shall have the same legal effect as a handwritten signature. For a digital signature to be legally valid, it must be: uniquely linked to the person signing; capable of identifying the person; created using data that the signatory can maintain under his sole control; and linked to the data to which it relates in a manner that any subsequent change of the data is detectable. Digital signatures based on certificates issued by the Controller of Certifying Authorities shall be presumed valid.

provisions.title: Section 17. Controller of Certifying Authorities
provisions.content: The Government shall appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities to discharge the functions and exercise the powers conferred upon him by or under this Act. The Controller shall have the power to: exercise supervision over the activities of the Certifying Authorities; lay down the standards to be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; specify the qualifications and experience of employees of the Certifying Authorities; specify the conditions under which Certifying Authorities shall conduct their business; specify the contents of written, printed or visual materials and advertisements that may be distributed or used in respect of digital signature Certificates; specify the form and content of a digital signature Certificate and the key; specify the form and manner in which accounts shall be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; specify the terms and conditions for appointment of Arbiters and their qualifications.

provisions.title: Section 19. Certifying Authorities
provisions.content: The Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, authorize one or more Government departments or authorize such persons as it deems fit to be Certifying Authorities for the purpose of this Act. A Certifying Authority shall have the power to issue, renew or revoke digital signature certificates to subscribers in accordance with this Act. A Certifying Authority shall: (a) make use of hardware and software that is reasonably secure from intrusion and misuse; (b) provide a reasonable level of reliability in its services which are reasonably suited to the performance of intended functions; (c) adhere to security procedures to ensure that the secrecy and privacy of digital signatures are assured; (d) be the repository for all digital signature certificates issued under this Act. The Certifying Authorities shall disclose their certificate practice statements, policies and procedures to enable subscribers and third parties to assess the level of trust they can place in certificates issued by the authority.

provisions.title: Section 27. Duties of Subscribers
provisions.content: Every subscriber shall exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in his digital signature Certificate and take all steps to prevent its disclosure to a person not authorized to affix the digital signature of the subscriber. If the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the digital signature Certificate has been compromised, then, the subscriber shall communicate the same without any delay to the Certifying Authority in such a manner as prescribed by the Controller. A subscriber shall be liable for any digital signature created using the private key of his digital signature Certificate unless and until the subscriber has notified the Certifying Authority of the compromise of such key. The subscriber shall be bound by all digital signatures created using his private key and digital signature Certificate unless he has taken all reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized use of his private key.

provisions.title: Section 33. Electronic Governance
provisions.content: Where any law provides for: the filing of any form, application or any other document with any office, authority, body or agency owned or controlled by the appropriate Government in a particular manner; the issue or grant of any licence, permit, sanction or approval by whatever name called in a particular manner; the receipt or payment of money in a particular manner — then, notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment, as the case may be, is effected by means of such electronic form as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government. The Government shall endeavour to make electronic filing, payment and communication a standard mode of interaction between citizens and government agencies. Electronic receipts and certificates issued by government agencies shall have the same validity as their paper equivalents.

provisions.title: Section 67. E-Governance Policy
provisions.content: The Government shall, in order to promote and develop the use of information and communication technologies in government administration and public service delivery, adopt and implement an e-Governance policy. The e-Governance policy shall aim to: reduce the cost and improve the efficiency of government operations through the use of electronic means; improve the quality and accessibility of public services through online delivery channels; promote transparency and accountability through electronic publication of government information; enable secure electronic transactions between citizens, businesses, and government agencies; promote paperless government operations through the use of electronic records and digital signatures; establish interoperability standards for government information systems; and develop the digital infrastructure and skills necessary for effective e-governance. The implementation of e-governance shall be coordinated by the Ministry of Information and Communication Technology.

provisions.title: Section 76. Cyber Appellate Tribunal
provisions.content: The Government shall establish one or more Cyber Appellate Tribunals to hear appeals from orders of the Controller of Certifying Authorities and from the Adjudicating Officers under this Act. A Cyber Appellate Tribunal shall consist of one person only, who shall be appointed by the Central Government. The presiding officer of a Cyber Appellate Tribunal shall be qualified to be a Judge of the High Court Division. The Tribunal shall not be bound by the procedure laid down by the Code of Civil Procedure, but shall be guided by the principles of natural justice and shall have powers to regulate its own procedure. A person aggrieved by the order of the Adjudicating Officer may prefer an appeal to the Cyber Appellate Tribunal within a period of forty-five days from the date of receipt of the order. An appeal against a decision of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal lies to the High Court Division of the Supreme Court.

provisions.title: Section 82. Penalty for Breach of Confidentiality and Privacy
provisions.content: Any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred under this Act, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such material to any other person shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to two lakh taka, or with both. This section protects the privacy of persons whose digital records and electronic communications may be accessed by Certifying Authorities, Adjudicating Officers, or other officials in the exercise of their powers under this Act. Persons authorised to access electronic records in the course of their official duties shall maintain strict confidentiality and shall not disclose information obtained to unauthorised persons. The offence under this section is cognizable and shall be investigated in accordance with the Code of Criminal Procedure.

definitions.definition: An authentication technique whereby a private key is used to create a digital code that, when transmitted along with an electronic record, can be verified using the signatory's public key to confirm that the record was signed by the holder of the private key and has not been altered.
A person or Government department authorized to issue digital signature certificates to subscribers, maintaining the infrastructure for the verification of digital signatures.
Data, record or data generated, image or sound stored, received or sent in an electronic form or micro film or computer generated micro fiche.
The Government-appointed officer responsible for supervising Certifying Authorities, setting standards, and administering the framework for digital signatures in Bangladesh.
